title: The Sword & Sorcery Saga: The Maze of Peril, by John Eric Holmes
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rJeHPb9VWP4

Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews *The Maze of Peril* by John Eric Holmes, a work that reads authentically like player characters adventuring around a D&D table. This novella captures the gleeful chaos of tabletop roleplaying—poor decisions, goofy moments, and the exuberant unpredictability of actual play. The cast includes Boinger the halfling fighter, Xerath the dark elf, Murray the wizard calling himself "second level," a knight named Sir Jeffrey who speaks questionable pseudo-English, Brother Ambrose the Christian cleric, and an amazon warrior riding a unicorn.

Holmes, instrumental in early D&D development, crafted characters that feel like actual player creations, complete with anachronistic gaming terminology sprinkled throughout. The book shines brightest during its urban adventure sequences—two chapters depicting a hilariously botched burglary involving a basilisk weapon and an accidentally petrified flesh golem are genuinely entertaining and full of childlike wonder.

The dungeon-crawling bookends feel less inspired, and the pacing sags toward the novel's end despite its short length. Yet *The Maze of Peril* succeeds precisely because it refuses polish; it reads like genuine table talk transcribed into prose, complete with warts and illogical player choices that make it feel authentic.

**How does *The Maze of Peril's* transparency about its D&D origins compare to other fantasy novels that draw from tabletop play?**
